meta.title: Kitsoki — adding a pet to the trace column, end to end

theme.name: rose-pine-moon

personas.name: Priya
personas.intro: Turns a one-line idea into a validated PRD and a mockup deck.

personas.name: Arden
personas.intro: Shapes requirements into a design epic, slices, and ADRs.

personas.name: Andy
personas.intro: Decomposes, fixes a mid-stream bug, refines, and ships — every step traceable.

scenes.title: Adding a pet to the trace column
scenes.subtitle: A dev story, end to end — idea to merged PR, every artifact a deck
scenes.narration: This is one dev story end to end: adding a small pet to the trace column — from a product idea to a merged pull request, where every phase produces a validated, replayable artifact.

scenes.title: The cast
scenes.caption: Three roles, one shared, traceable pipeline — every hand-off a notification, every artifact a validated, replayable output.
scenes.narration: A product manager, an architect, and a developer share one traceable pipeline. Every hand-off is a notification, and every artifact is a validated, replayable output.

scenes.title: Who does what

cases.action: Frames the pet idea into a PRD + mockup
Turns the requirements into a design
Decomposes the design into a work plan
Fixes a bug that lands mid-stream
Fixes, validates, and ships the feature
Opens the PR with a demo of the live pet

scenes.caption: Three roles, one shared, traceable pipeline — every hand-off a notification, every artifact a validated deck.
scenes.narration: Across the story, the product manager frames the pet idea, the architect designs it, and the developer decomposes it, fixes a bug, ships the feature, and opens the pull request with a demo of the live pet.

scenes.title: It starts on GitHub
scenes.subtitle: A real @kitsoki mention on a labelled issue → a run link in the reply → the kitsoki run page
scenes.narration: Before any of this reaches the kitsoki composer, it starts where the work already lives: a real at-kitsoki mention on a labelled GitHub issue. The label decides the route, kitsoki replies with a run link, and that link opens the hosted run page.

scenes.title: Bug label → bugfix
scenes.caption: @kitsoki on a bug-labelled issue → stories/bugfix. Live: github.com/bsacrobatix/Kitsoki/issues/44
scenes.narration: A bug-labelled mention routes straight to the bugfix story — one mention, one claimed job, one run link.

scenes.title: Feature label → dev story
scenes.caption: @kitsoki on a feature-labelled issue → the dev story that follows. Live: issues/45
scenes.narration: A feature-labelled mention routes to the dev story you are about to watch — the same loop, opened from GitHub.

scenes.title: Click it → the kitsoki run page
scenes.caption: The run link opens the hosted kitsoki page: origin, a source link back to the issue, story, state, timeline
scenes.narration: The run link opens a hosted run page — origin, a source link back to the exact issue, the story, the state, and a full timeline.

scenes.title: …and the same run as JSON
scenes.caption: The same job as JSON — every run is queryable, not just viewable
scenes.narration: And the same job is available as JSON, so every run is queryable, not just viewable.

scenes.title: 1 · Idea → PRD + mockup

cases.action: Types a one-line idea: a pet for the trace column
Answers clarifying questions; a PRD is drafted
Publishes the validated PRD + mockup

scenes.caption: The PRD and mockup are the validated output of the product-manager phase.
scenes.narration: The product manager starts with a one-line idea: a little pet for the trace column. Kitsoki runs a conversation-driven intake — clarifying questions, a drafted PRD, and a mockup — and the PRD is the validated output.

scenes.title: A little pet for the trace column
scenes.subtitle: Give the long agent-loop wait some life — without taking screen space
scenes.narration: The product manager's validated output: a PRD for a small companion that lives in the trace column.

scenes.lede: Watching a long run is a lonely wait.
scenes.body: The trace column just ticks rows by while the agent thinks, tests run, and a fix lands. Nothing on the screen is alive. A small ambient companion can add warmth to that wait — and cost nothing.
scenes.narration: Watching a long session is a lonely wait at the trace column. Nothing on screen is alive. A small ambient companion adds warmth to that wait, and costs nothing.

scenes.title: What it is
scenes.narration: One small animated S-V-G pet, anchored at the bottom of the trace column. It idles, blinks, wanders, and now and then a snack appears and it wanders over to eat it.

cards.label: Lives at the column bottom
cards.sub: anchored to .iv__trace, never displaces a row

cards.label: Idles & plays
cards.sub: bobs, blinks, wanders, the occasional snack & poop

cards.label: Opt-in
cards.sub: off by default; one toggle turns it on

cards.label: Zero weight
cards.sub: inline SVG + CSS keyframes — no images, no deps, no network

scenes.title: Goals & guardrails
scenes.narration: The goals are simple and the guardrails are strict: opt-in, ambient, and weightless.

cells: A small animated SVG pet at the trace-column bottom
Absolutely positioned — never blocks or displaces trace rows
Pure inline SVG + CSS — no images, deps, or network

scenes.body: A published PRD and an HTML mockup of the pet in the real drive layout — the validated product output that the architect picks up next.
scenes.narration: The validated output: a published PRD and an HTML mockup of the pet in the real drive layout, ready for the architect to pick up.

messages.author: github

Design issue assigned to Arden — requirements ready ✅

scenes.caption: Every hand-off is a notification where the work lives — mocked here for the story.
scenes.narration: The PRD is published, and a design issue is assigned to the architect: requirements ready.

scenes.title: GitHub · Design Ticket #52
scenes.caption: The live design issue #52 on GitHub with the slidey design deck linked as an asset.
scenes.narration: Arden receives the notification and goes to the live GitHub issue #52 to review the design deck linked directly in the issue body.

scenes.title: 2 · Requirements → design

cases.action: Reviews the new PRD and asks questions
Produces slices, a data flow, and ADRs
Publishes the design as a slidey deck

scenes.caption: The design — three additive slices, a leaf data flow, and ADRs — is the architect phase's validated output.
scenes.narration: The architect is notified that requirements are ready, reviews them, and produces the design — three additive slices, a leaf data flow, and decisions that keep the feature weightless.

scenes.title: Designing the trace-column pet
scenes.subtitle: A self-contained sprite, a dock seam, and an opt-in toggle — zero new state, zero deps
scenes.narration: The architect's validated output: a design for the trace-column pet — a self-contained sprite, a dock seam, and an opt-in toggle, with zero new state and zero dependencies.

scenes.lede: Derive nothing new — dock at the column bottom.
scenes.body: The pet introduces no new application state and reads no trace data. It is a purely decorative component, absolutely positioned at the bottom of the existing trace column, so it overlays the footer instead of joining the row flow. Three small, additive pieces: a sprite component, a dock seam, and an opt-in setting.
scenes.narration: The design derives nothing from new state and reads no trace data. The pet is purely decorative, absolutely positioned at the bottom of the existing trace column so it overlays the footer instead of joining the row flow. Three small additive pieces: a sprite component, a dock seam, and an opt-in setting.

scenes.title: The three slices
scenes.narration: Slice one is the sprite component: TracePet dot vue, inline S-V-G with a C-S-S idle loop. Slice two is the dock seam, anchoring it at the bottom of the trace column without displacing rows. Slice three is the opt-in setting, off by default, that gates whether the pet mounts.

cards.label: Slice 1 · Sprite
cards.sub: TracePet.vue — inline SVG + CSS idle loop (bob, blink, wander, poop, eat); no props, no deps

cards.label: Slice 2 · Dock seam
cards.sub: anchor in InteractiveView.vue .iv__trace; absolutely positioned over the footer, never displaces rows

cards.label: Slice 3 · Opt-in
cards.sub: persisted boolean, default false; 🐾 toggle in the State Diagram header mounts the pet

scenes.title: Data flow
scenes.narration: The data flow is a straight line: the trace-column layout hosts the TracePet, anchored at its bottom, and TracePet renders an inline S-V-G sprite. Nothing flows the other way — the pet is a leaf.

cards.label: Trace column layout
cards.sub: .iv__trace — position: relative host

cards.label: → TracePet anchored bottom
cards.sub: absolute, overlays the column footer

cards.label: → inline SVG sprite
cards.sub: CSS keyframes; decorative leaf, no data in or out

scenes.title: Decisions (ADRs)
scenes.narration: Two anchoring decisions. First: inline S-V-G plus C-S-S keyframes only, so the feature ships zero assets and cannot slow the U-I. Second: opt-in and absolutely positioned at the column bottom, so it can never push, clip, or block a trace row. Reduced-motion is honored throughout.

cells: No images, sprite sheets, libraries, or network — ships zero assets, can't slow the UI
Off by default; overlays the footer so it never pushes, clips, or blocks a trace row
Dampen/pause the bob & blink; pet is decorative (aria-hidden)

scenes.body: A published design: three additive slices, a leaf data flow, and decisions that keep the feature deterministic and weightless — true to kitsoki's no-deps spirit. The validated output the developer decomposes into a sequenced work plan next.
scenes.narration: The validated output: a published design with three additive slices, a leaf data flow, and decisions that keep the feature deterministic and weightless — true to kitsoki's no-dependencies spirit. The developer decomposes this into a sequenced work plan next.

scenes.title: 3 · Design → work plan

cases.action: Reviews the published design
Decomposes the slices into right-sized tasks

scenes.caption: The decomposed, dependency-ordered work plan is the output.
scenes.narration: The developer reviews the new design and decomposes its slices into right-sized, dependency-ordered tasks. The validated work plan is the output.

scenes.title: Decomposing the pet into a work plan
scenes.subtitle: Three design slices, sequenced into concrete, shippable tasks
scenes.narration: The developer's validated output: the trace-column pet design turned into a sequenced work plan — three slices broken into concrete, shippable tasks.

scenes.lede: Turn the design into a sequence.
scenes.body: Each design slice becomes one or two concrete tasks, ordered so every step is independently verifiable. Build the sprite first, dock it second, gate it behind the opt-in third — then typecheck, document, and changelog. Purely additive, no migration.
scenes.narration: Each design slice becomes one or two concrete tasks, ordered so every step is independently verifiable. Build the sprite first, dock it second, gate it behind the opt-in third — then typecheck, document, and changelog. Purely additive, no migration.

scenes.title: Tasks
scenes.narration: Slice one: author TracePet dot vue with the idle, wander, poop, and eat behaviour loop, plus a reduced-motion gate. Slice two: dock it in InteractiveView dot vue's trace column, made position-relative, never displacing rows. Slice three: a localStorage opt-in pref and the paw toggle in the panel header. Then typecheck and build, document the setting, and add a changelog entry.

cells: Add prefers-reduced-motion gate to the sprite
Dock in InteractiveView.vue .iv__trace (position:relative, never displace rows)
🐾 toggle in the State Diagram panel header
Document the setting in the web UI reference

scenes.title: Sequencing & dependencies
scenes.narration: The order is gated by dependencies. The sprite must exist before it can be docked; the dock must exist before the toggle can mount it. Verification, docs, and changelog ride on top once the three slices land.

cards.label: 1 · Sprite (standalone)
cards.sub: no dependencies — author & unit-render first

cards.label: 2 · Dock (needs Sprite)
cards.sub: mount point depends on TracePet existing

cards.label: 3 · Opt-in (needs Dock)
cards.sub: toggle gates the mounted dock

cards.label: 4 · Verify · Docs · Changelog
cards.sub: typecheck/build, reference, changelog after slices land

scenes.lede: Work plan validated — ready to implement.
scenes.body: A sequenced, dependency-ordered plan where every task is small, additive, and independently verifiable. The validated output, ready to implement in the background.
scenes.narration: The validated output: a sequenced, dependency-ordered plan where every task is small, additive, and independently verifiable — ready to implement in the background.

scenes.title: A bug lands

messages.author: github
messages.body:
Bug filed & assigned to Andy — redundant Observe ↗ link 🐞
https://github.com/bsacrobatix/Kitsoki/issues/54

scenes.caption: Every hand-off is a notification where the work lives — mocked here for the story.
scenes.narration: Mid-stream, a bug is filed and assigned to the developer: a redundant Observe link cluttering the topbar.

scenes.title: GitHub · Bug Ticket #54
scenes.caption: The live bug ticket #54 on GitHub reporting the redundant link.
scenes.narration: Andy receives a bug notification and inspects the live GitHub issue #54, containing the bug report deck and reproduction replay.

scenes.title: 4 · A bug lands mid-stream

cases.action: Reviews the bug report and its rrweb repro
Triage recommends a one-shot, unsupervised fix
Confirms scope before the fix runs

scenes.caption: While the work plan runs, a real kitsoki bug is reported with a captured repro.
scenes.narration: Mid-stream, a real bug is reported against the kitsoki web UI — a redundant Observe link that gets confused with the trace toggle. The report carries an rrweb repro.

scenes.title: A redundant Observe ↗ link
scenes.subtitle: It sits beside the trace toggle and gets confused with it
scenes.narration: A small clutter bug: a redundant Observe link in the topbar, sitting right next to the trace toggle and easily mistaken for it.

scenes.lede: Two controls that look like one job.
scenes.body: The InteractiveView topbar carries an 'Observe ↗' router-link directly next to the Hide / show-trace toggle. The toggle controls the trace column you're already looking at; the link navigates away to the read-only observer for the very same run. Same neighbourhood, near-identical affordance — so people reach for one expecting the other, click out of their live session, and have to navigate back. It's pure clutter against a control it's easily confused with.
scenes.narration: The topbar puts an Observe link right next to the trace toggle. The toggle shows or hides the trace you're already on; the link jumps to the read-only observer for the same run. They look alike, they sit together, and people click the wrong one — leaving their live session for no reason. It's clutter.

scenes.title: InteractiveView.vue · the offending markup

annotations.text: Lives in the topbar at InteractiveView.vue:85, beside the trace toggle
Navigates to the read-only observer for the same run — a dead-end relative to the live view

scenes.caption: tools/runstatus/src/views/InteractiveView.vue:85 — plus its CSS at ~937–946.
scenes.narration: Here it is: a single router-link at InteractiveView line 85, with its own CSS just below. It points at the observer route for the run you're already watching live.

scenes.title: Reproduce & impact

cards.label: Open any kitsoki web run
cards.sub: the live InteractiveView with its trace column

cards.label: Look at the topbar
cards.sub: the Observe ↗ link sits right next to the trace toggle

cards.label: Reach for the toggle, hit the link
cards.sub: you're navigated out to the read-only observer of the same run

scenes.caption: Impact: confusable controls, an accidental trip out of the live session, zero added value.
scenes.narration: To see it: open any web run, glance at the topbar, and reach for the trace toggle — the look-alike Observe link is right there to catch the misclick, sending you out to a read-only view of the run you're already on.

scenes.body: The report ships an rrweb repro — a deterministic browser replay showing the two confusable controls in the live topbar. The recommended fix is trivial and self-contained: delete the link at InteractiveView.vue:85 and its CSS rule (~937–946). No behaviour depends on it; the observer route stays reachable elsewhere. Triage recommendation: one-shot, unsupervised.
scenes.narration: The report carries an rrweb repro that replays the confusable topbar. Because the fix is a self-contained deletion with nothing depending on it, this is a clean one-shot, unsupervised candidate.

scenes.title: Triaged → go
scenes.caption: Every hand-off is a notification where the work lives — mocked here for the story.
scenes.narration: Triage clears it as a clean one-shot, unsupervised fix. The developer says go.

scenes.title: 5 · Fix & validate

cases.action: Kitsoki reproduces, fixes, and self-reviews
Runs the real checks — typecheck + build
Validates the fix — PR-ready

scenes.caption: The bug-fix room reproduces, fixes, tests, and validates — closing out PR-ready.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki reproduces the bug, makes a minimal deletion, runs the real typecheck and build, and the rrweb replay confirms the clutter is gone — closing out ready for a pull request.

scenes.title: Remove the redundant Observe link
scenes.subtitle: One control, one job — just the trace toggle remains
scenes.narration: The fix: remove the redundant Observe link so the topbar keeps a single, unambiguous control — the trace toggle.

scenes.body: The whole change is a removal: delete the 'Observe ↗' router-link at InteractiveView.vue:85, then delete its now-orphaned CSS rule (.iv__observe-link, lines ~937–946). Nothing else references the element, and the observer route stays reachable from elsewhere — so no markup or routing needs to move. The confusable second affordance simply disappears.
scenes.narration: It's a deletion. Remove the Observe link at line 85, and its orphaned CSS just below. Nothing depends on the element and the observer route stays reachable, so nothing else has to change.

scenes.title: Before vs after

right.label: After
lines: no accidental trips to the observer

scenes.caption: From two confusable controls to one unambiguous toggle.
scenes.narration: Before: two confusable controls sharing the topbar. After: just the trace toggle — one control with one clear job, and no accidental trips out of the live view.

scenes.title: Validation — green after the fix

cells: clean — no dangling references to iv__observe-link
green — bundle builds with the element and CSS removed
the Observe link is gone; the trace toggle stands alone

scenes.caption: Typecheck and build pass, and the captured rrweb replay confirms the link no longer renders in the topbar.

scenes.body: The redundant control is gone, the typechecker and build are green, and the rrweb replay shows the topbar with the trace toggle alone — no stray Observe link to misclick. A clean, minimal diff: ready to open the PR.
scenes.narration: The clutter is gone, typecheck and build are green, and the replay shows a clean topbar. Minimal diff, ready to open the pull request.

scenes.title: 6 · Ship the feature

cases.action: Implements the three slices in the background
Reviews the demo of the shipped pet
Opens the PR with the demo deck

scenes.caption: The pet ships in three slices — then the real, live pet closes the story.
scenes.narration: The work plan moves into background development, and the three slices land: the sprite, the dock, and the opt-in toggle. Then the real pet, captured live, closes the story.

scenes.title: Meet Kit — the trace-column pet
scenes.subtitle: A little life at the bottom of the trace, opt-in and never in the way
scenes.narration: Meet Kit: a small animated companion that lives at the bottom of the trace column — opt-in, and never in your way.

scenes.lede: A tiny companion for the long wait.
scenes.body: Watching a long agent loop is a lonely wait — rows tick by while the agent thinks, tests run, and a fix lands. Kit is a small decorative SVG pet anchored at the bottom of the trace column that idles and plays through that wait. It's a single inline-SVG component, absolutely positioned, so it adds warmth without ever displacing a trace row.
scenes.narration: Watching a long run is a lonely wait. Kit fills it with a little life — a small SVG pet at the bottom of the trace column that idles and plays while the agent works, without ever pushing a row out of the way.

scenes.title: What Kit does
scenes.narration: Kit wanders along the floor of the column, blinks, leaves the occasional poop, and now and then a snack appears and it strolls over to eat it. It lives at the bottom of the trace, you opt in with a single paw-print toggle, and it costs nothing — pure inline S-V-G and C-S-S.

cards.label: Wanders & blinks
cards.sub: bobs along the column floor, blinks now and then

cards.label: Poops & snacks
cards.sub: leaves the occasional poop; walks over to eat a snack

cards.label: Lives at the trace bottom
cards.sub: docked in .iv__trace, absolutely positioned, never displaces a row

cards.label: Opt-in 🐾 toggle
cards.sub: off by default; one paw-print toggle in the State Diagram header

cards.label: Zero weight
cards.sub: pure inline SVG + CSS keyframes — no images, no deps, no network

scenes.title: Delivered in three slices

cells: TracePet.vue — inline SVG + CSS keyframes for idle, wander, blink, poop, and snack
Docked at the bottom of .iv__trace in InteractiveView.vue, absolutely positioned over the column floor
A 🐾 toggle in the State Diagram header — off by default, persists the choice

scenes.caption: Three small slices: the component, where it lives, and how you turn it on.

scenes.body: Kit is deliberately weightless: no images, no dependencies, no network — just inline SVG and CSS keyframes. It's absolutely positioned so it can never block or shift a trace row, it's off by default, and it respects reduced-motion. A little personality, held to strict guardrails.
scenes.narration: Kit is charming but disciplined — no images, no dependencies, no network, just inline S-V-G and C-S-S. It can't block a trace row, it's off by default, and it respects reduced-motion.

scenes.lede: A little life in the trace column.
scenes.body: Flip the 🐾 toggle in the State Diagram header and Kit wakes up at the bottom of your trace — wandering, blinking, and snacking through the wait. Opt-in, weightless, and never in the way.
scenes.narration: Flip the paw-print toggle and Kit wakes up at the bottom of your trace. A little life in the trace column — opt-in, weightless, and never in the way.

scenes.title: Kit, wandering in the trace column
scenes.caption: A real rrweb capture of the shipped pet docked at the bottom of .iv__trace — opt-in via the 🐾 toggle
scenes.narration: And here is the real, shipped pet — captured live from the kitsoki web UI. Flip the paw-print toggle and Kit wakes up at the bottom of the trace column, wandering through the wait.

And the whole story is a no-LLM, byte-deterministic replay: every phase a validated artifact, every hand-off a notification, the live pet a real capture.
